text: The retrohepatic cavell dissection for a robotic right hepatectomy seems intimidating, but actually with the robotic visualization and articulating instruments, it can be performed similar to an open right hepatectomy.
My name is Jason Hawksworth, and I'm a transplant and hepatobiliary surgeon.
In this brief video, I'll demonstrate how we perform the retrohepatic cavell dissection for a robotic right hepatectomy.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm equipped with a Raytech to mobilize the liver, allowing for the division of the hepatorenal ligament.
The surgeon sequentially divides the short hepatic vein branches as they come into view, preventing inadvertent tearing while medializing the liver during the robotic right hepatectomy.
The surgeon divides smaller short hepatic veins using robotic metal clips or a robotic vessel sealer, demonstrating the options for vessel management during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the triangular ligament, continuing the mobilization of the liver using robotic instruments.
The surgeon controls and divides the short hepatic vein branches as they come into view during the robotic right hepatectomy, ensuring careful separation to prevent tearing.
The surgeon uses hemlock clips to divide larger short hepatic vein branches, ensuring controlled separation as the liver is medialized.
The surgeon divides the triangular ligaments, progressing towards the right hepatic vein, facilitating further liver mobilization.
This is sometimes easier by flipping the camera in the thirty degree up position or even placing the camera through robotic port number one for better medial visualization.
During the retrohepatic caval dissection, the surgeon identifies and exposes the inferior hepatic vein accessory branches, which are commonly observed in this area.
The surgeon controls and divides the short hepatic vein branches using hemlock clips, ensuring secure ligation of these vessels during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide larger short hepatic vein branches, demonstrating an alternative method to hemlock clips for vessel control.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the liver, revealing the base of the right hepatic vein, which is typically adequate for completing a robotic right hepatectomy.
The surgeon dissects around the extrahepatic right hepatic vein, which is located near the inferior vena cava, although this step is typically not necessary unless a lesion is present.
At the completion of the dissection, a Raytech sponge is placed just lateral to the inferior vena cava, marking the end of the surgical exposure in this area.
And this can be used later to ensure a proper plane of transection during liver division.
Stay tuned for our next video demonstrating parenchymal division during a robotic right hepatectomy.
We hope you enjoyed this video and have a great day.
